description: Scotland's most dramatic island — jagged Cuillin peaks, fairy pools, ancient castles, and a landscape that has inspired painters, poets, and filmmakers for centuries.

pois.name: Old Man of Storr
pois.summary: A 50m pinnacle of crumbling basalt that seems to defy gravity — the most photographed rock formation in Scotland.

trivia.title: Prometheus (2012)
trivia.body: Ridley Scott opened his Alien prequel Prometheus with sweeping aerial shots of the Old Man of Storr and the Trotternish Ridge, representing a primeval Earth landscape. The same area also appeared in Macbeth (2015) starring Michael Fassbender, and several scenes of The BFG (2016) were set against Skye's extraordinary skyline. The island's otherworldly rock formations make it one of the most frequently used locations in British cinema.

media.caption: The Old Man of Storr on the Trotternish Peninsula

trivia.title: First climbed in 1955
trivia.body: The Old Man of Storr appears completely unclimbable — a leaning, crumbling pinnacle of rotten basalt with no obvious route to the top. English mountaineer Don Whillans first reached the summit in 1955, a feat that has been repeated only a handful of times since. The pinnacle is formed by a massive ancient landslide and is slowly continuing to move.

pois.name: The Quiraing
pois.summary: A surreal landscape of land-slipped cliffs, pinnacles, and hidden plateaux on Skye's Trotternish Peninsula.

trivia.title: A landscape from another world
trivia.body: The Quiraing's strange topography of leaning pinnacles, hidden valleys, and tilting grassy platforms has appeared in numerous productions including Stardust (2007), Transformers: The Last Knight (2017), and most recently season 3 of BBC's Shetland. The road that crosses the ridge between Staffin and Uig is one of the most dramatic drives in the UK — and frequently closed in winter when the road itself continues to slip.

media.caption: The Quiraing — Skye's most dramatic landscape

trivia.title: Still moving today
trivia.body: The entire Trotternish Ridge is the result of a massive ongoing landslide — the longest in the UK. The basalt lava that caps the peninsula is sliding slowly over the softer sedimentary rocks below, creating the chaotic landscape of pinnacles, mesas, and tilted slabs you see today. The road over the Quiraing regularly needs realigning as the ground beneath it continues to shift.

pois.name: Dunvegan Castle
pois.summary: The ancient seat of Clan MacLeod — one of the oldest continuously inhabited castles in Scotland, home to the legendary Fairy Flag.

trivia.title: The Fairy Flag
trivia.body: Dunvegan Castle's most prized possession is the Fairy Flag — a fragile, yellowed silk banner that scientific analysis dates to somewhere between the 4th and 7th centuries, possibly from the Middle East. Clan MacLeod legend holds that it was given to a MacLeod chief by his fairy wife, and that waving it in battle will save the clan from defeat — but only three times. It has supposedly been waved twice already. During WWII, many MacLeod men carried photographs of it into battle.

media.caption: Dunvegan Castle, seat of Clan MacLeod

trivia.title: The drinking horn challenge
trivia.body: Among Dunvegan's treasures is Rory Mor's Drinking Horn — a beautiful 16th-century vessel of Celtic design that holds 2.2 litres of claret. According to clan tradition, each new MacLeod chief must drink the entire contents in one go 'without setting down or falling down'. In 1956, the 29th chief John MacLeod accomplished this in 1 minute and 57 seconds, thereby maintaining the tradition unbroken.

trivia.title: Bonnie Prince Charlie's keepsakes
trivia.body: Bonnie Prince Charlie's waistcoat and a lock of his hair are displayed at Dunvegan — donated by Flora MacDonald's granddaughter. Flora MacDonald herself visited the castle after helping the Prince escape 'over the sea to Skye' disguised as her Irish maid 'Betty Burke' in 1746. The Prince was an unconvincing woman: described by one witness as 'an odd muckle trallup of a carlin', and caused alarm by his wide stride and unshaven face.

pois.name: Fairy Pools
pois.summary: A series of crystal-clear, ice-cold pools and waterfalls at the foot of the Black Cuillin — one of the most visited spots in all of Scotland.

trivia.title: The Black Cuillin
trivia.body: The pools are fed by streams from the Black Cuillin ridge — a jagged arc of gabbro and basalt that forms the most challenging mountain terrain in the British Isles. The rock is so rough it grips boot rubber perfectly, but the ridges are genuinely technical and require ropes. The Cuillin has been described as the only true mountaineering in Britain — several peaks remained unclimbed until the early 20th century.

media.caption: The Fairy Pools below the Black Cuillin

trivia.title: Wild swimming capital
trivia.body: The Fairy Pools are one of Scotland's most popular wild swimming spots — the water is extraordinarily clear but bitterly cold, even in summer (typically 8-12°C). The pools are on the River Brittle near Glenbrittle, a 20-minute walk from the car park. Arrive early or late to avoid the worst of the summer crowds, which can number in the thousands on peak days.

pois.name: Portree
pois.summary: Skye's largest town and capital — a pretty harbour ringed by colourful painted houses, named 'King's Harbour' after a royal visit in 1540.

trivia.title: Flora MacDonald's farewell
trivia.body: Portree Harbour is traditionally the spot where Bonnie Prince Charlie said farewell to Flora MacDonald in 1746 after she had smuggled him across from the mainland disguised as her maid. He reportedly told her: 'For all that has happened, I hope, Madam, we shall meet in St James's yet'. They never met again. Flora was arrested shortly afterwards and held in the Tower of London for a year before being released without trial.

media.caption: Portree harbour with its colourful painted houses

trivia.title: King's Harbour
trivia.body: Portree takes its name from the Gaelic 'Port Righ' — King's Harbour — commemorating a visit by King James V in 1540. James sailed to the Hebrides with a fleet of twelve warships to assert royal authority over the island chiefs, many of whom were essentially independent rulers. The visit was more diplomatic than military, though James arrested several chiefs briefly to make his point.
